rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Number Two (First Amended) And Request For Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 3 of 3) Lucas's request for sanctions is DENIED. Lucas bases his request on Code of Civil Procedure sections 2023.010 and 2023.020. The court finds that Lucas has not shown that Ballonoff failed to meet and confer in reasonably good faith, or otherwise abused the discovery process. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2023.010 & 2023.020.) Lucas shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on May 12, 2022. Remote appearance is encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. Any oral argument will be limited to 15 minutes per side.

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Number Two (First Amended) And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 1 of 3) Marilyn Silverman-Ballonoff is the subject of a conservatorship. The conservator of her person and estate is Thomas Lucas. Conservators of estates must periodically-generally, every two years-provide the court with an accounting that reports on activity and accounts for receipts and expenditures. The requirements for accountings are set out in the Probate Code and the Rules of Court, as well as in local rules. A conservatorship accounting is generally made up of a petition that contains a report on activities, financial schedules, statements and receipts. The accounting, itself, is a type of discovery in that it compels the trustee to provide extensive information to all interested parties. Those disclosures can lead to discussions and even other adversarial proceedings and petitions. Pending before the court is Lucas's fourth conservatorship account petition. Silverman-Ballonoff's daughter, Selene Ballonoff, has filed objections to Lucas's fourth account. Now before the Court is Objector Ballonoff's Motion for an Order Compelling Conservator Thomas Lucas: 1) "to serve on her a response to the Objector's Requests for Production of Documents, Set One [sic], which she served on Conservator Tom Lucas on September 23, 2021;" and 2) "to pay a monetary sanction to Objector." Conservator Thomas Lucas opposes the motion in its entirety and requests that monetary sanctions be awarded to him. Ballonoff's motion is DENIED, as is her request for sanctions. Lucas's request for sanctions is DENIED. Ballonoff's motion is denied on several independent grounds. First, to the extent Ballonoff moves for an order directing Lucas "to serve on her a response to the Objector's Requests for Production of Documents, Set One [sic], which she served on Conservator Tom Lucas on September 23, 2021," the motion is denied because Lucas served responses. Ballonoff's grievance is, in fact, the responses were inadequate. Notably, the discovery vehicle at issue is Ballonoff's Requests for Production of Documents, Set Two, and all subsequent references are to Ballonoff's document demand, set two. Second, to the extent Ballonoff requests an order directing Lucas to file verified responses without objections, the motion is denied. The issue is the timeliness of Lucas's response to Ballonoff's discovery demand. "The party making a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall serve a copy of the demand on the party to whom it is directed and on all other parties who have appeared in the action." (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.040.) Ballonoff failed to serve the party on whom the demand was directed. (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.040.) The POS, included as Attachment A to the motion, shows Ballonoff served Franchesca Callejo, who is Lucas's counsel in his capacity as conservator; and Charles Jonas, attorney for Silverman-Ballonoff. Ballonoff did not serve Vincent Yu, Lucas's separate litigation counsel, and Ballonoff was so advised. Had Ballonoff properly served Lucas with the discovery at issue, his responses would have been due on October 29, 2021. To the extent Lucas only became aware of the served discovery sometime in mid-October 2021 and served responses shortly thereafter on November 5, 2021, the Court finds the responses are timely. Ballonoff's objections under sections 2031.220, 2031.230 and 2031.240 are overruled. Ballonoff has not caried her burden of showing violations of the relevant statutes, nor has she demonstrated any prejudice from any claimed violation.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Number Two (First Amended) And Request For Monetary Sanctions (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 2 of 3) Third, Ballonoff's request for further responses to her document demand, set two is denied because she failed to carry her initial burden. The moving party must make a showing of good cause justifying the discovery sought by the document demand. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2031.310, subd. (b)(1).) The court finds that Ballonoff has failed to demonstrate good cause for the discovery sought by her document demand, set two. Ballonoff's conclusory statement that good cause exists does not establish that, in fact, good cause exists for her request in set two. Fourth, Ballonoff's demand for a further response in the form of a "copy-and-pastable, searchable PDF, format-intact electronic" copy of the fourth account, itself, and portions of the third conservatorship account is denied. Ballonoff starts from the premise that the accounts are ESI, or "electronically stored information," under the discovery statutes. The court finds that the third and fourth accounting petitions are not ESI. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2016.020, subd. (e).) ESI is, for example, inventory information that is maintained with other information in a complex digital data base; often the issue is the accessibility of the information sought. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2031.210, subd. (d) & 2031.310, subd. (d).) The third and fourth accounting petitions, by contrast, are made of a report and schedules prepared by counsel and supporting documents taken from different sources, such as banks and vendors. Ballonoff has presented no material evidence that Lucas maintains these materials as ESI. Accordingly, Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.030(a)(2), which authorizes a party to specify the form in which the electronically stored information is to be produced, is not applicable. To the extent Lucas has a pdf file of the fourth account, it has been produced, as have hard copies of both accounts. Ballonoff has not provided any authority, and the court has not found any, to support the proposition that a responding party is required to convert third-party documents into a form different than that in which the producing party maintains them, just to download them and produce them to another party's preferred format. Indeed, the relevant authority indicates that the responding party must have ESI in its "possession, custody, or control" at the time of the request, not that the responding party must convert information into ESI in order to produce it to the demanding party in a particular form. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2016.020, subd. (e).) To impose such a burden on Lucas is to unnecessarily tax Silverman-Ballonoff's resources and would amount to waste. Lucas' objection with respect to the request for Trust accounting information in this conservatorship proceeding is sustained. Ballonoff's request for sanctions is DENIED. Subject to exceptions not relevant here, "the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes  a motion to compel further response to a demand, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.310, subd. (h).) Ballonoff's motion was unsuccessful. Thus, there is no basis to award sanctions to her.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: MARILYN SILVERMAN-BALLONOFF REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Number One And Request For Monetary Sanctions (Added For The Purpose Of Posting Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 2 of 3) Third, Ballonoff's request for further responses to her document demand, set one, is denied because she failed to carry her initial burden. The moving party must make an initial showing of good cause justifying the discovery sought by the document demand. (See Code Civ. Proc., 2031.310, subd. (b)(1).) The court finds that Ballonoff has failed to demonstrate good cause for the discovery sought by her document demand, set one. Ballonoffs conclusory statement that good cause exists does not establish that, in fact, good cause exists for any-let alone all-of the requests in her document demand, set one. Fourth, Ballonoff's demand for further responses on each of her four demands presented in set one fails. Ballonoff already has in her possession Lucass umbrella insurance policy and, therefore, the court will not order Lucas to produce it. Lucas objection to producing his personal auto insurance, home insurance, health insurance, and long-term care insurance on relevance grounds is sustained. Nor will the court order Lucas to produce bonds and insurance policies held by Franchesca Callejo, Sheila Robello, Natalie Lupo, Vincent Yu, Daniel Conrad, Elizabeth Hanowsky and/or Solan, Park & Robello. Lucas states he has no access to such documents, which makes sense, and Ballonoff presents no evidence to the contrary. Nor has Ballonoff satisfied her initial burden to show that the information is relevant to the second accounting petition. (See Glenfed Develop. Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (National Union Fire Ins. Co. of Pittsburgh, Penn.) (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 1113, 1117 [moving party must show both: (1) relevance to the subject matter (e.g., how the information in the documents would tend to prove or disprove some issue in the case); and (2) specific facts justifying discovery (e.g., why such information is necessary for trial preparation or to prevent surprise at trial)].) Nor will the court compel further responses to Ballonoffs request for all documents related to counsel Callejo's IOLTA accounts. Lucas has stated he has no access to this information, which makes sense, and Ballonoff has not presented any material evidence suggesting otherwise. Nor has Ballonoff satisfied her initial burden to show that the information is relevant to the second accounting petition. Ballonoff has failed to show that counsel Callejos business address notices to the United States Postal Service and the State Bar have any bearing on any issue raised by the second trust accounting and, therefore, the court will not compel further responses on this request. Ballonoff already has in her possession substantial evidence related to Trust expenditures; indeed, many invoices, expenditures, receipts, and records are in the accounting petition that was served on Ballonoff. Lucas reports that he has no other documents and Ballonoff has not presented any material evidence to the contrary. Lucass objection with respect to the Conservatorship accounting is sustained on the ground that it is not an appropriate request in the Trust matter. Ballonoff's request for sanctions is DENIED. Subject to exceptions not relevant here, "the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010 ) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes  a motion to compel further response to a demand, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.310, subd. (h).) Ballonoff's motion was unsuccessful. Thus, there is no basis to award sanctions to her.

rulings.Case Title: IN RE: MARILYN SILVERMAN-BALLONOFF REVOCABLE TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set Number One And Request For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: This tentative ruling is issued by Hon. Joseph M. Quinn. (Part 1 of 3) Marilyn Silverman-Ballonoff is the beneficiary of a court-created, court-supervised trust ("Trust"). Thomas Lucas is the trustee of the Trust. Where, as here, a trust is under court supervision, the trustee must periodically-generally, every two years-provide the court with an accounting that reports on activity and accounts for receipts and expenditures. The requirements for accountings are set out in the Probate Code and the Rules of Court, as well as in local rules. A trust accounting is generally made up of a petition that contains a report on activities, financial schedules, statements and receipts. The accounting is, itself, a discovery vehicle in that it compels the trustee to provide extensive information to all beneficiaries. Those disclosures can lead to discussions and even other adversarial proceedings and petitions. Pending before the court is Trustee Lucass second account petition. Selene Ballonoff, a contingent beneficiary of the Trust, objects. Now before the court is Objector Ballonoffs Motion for an Order Compelling Trustee Thomas Lucas: 1) "to serve on her a response to the Objectors Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, which she served on Trustee Tom Lucas on September 23, 2021;" and 2) "to pay a monetary sanction to Objector." Ballonoff moves under Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310. Trustee Thomas Lucas opposes the motion in its entirety and requests that monetary sanctions be awarded to him. Ballonoff's motion is DENIED, as is her request for sanctions. Lucas's request for sanctions is DENIED. Ballonoff's motion is denied on several independent grounds. First, to the extent Ballonoff moves for an order directing Lucas "to serve on her a response to the Objector's Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, which she served on Trustee Tom Lucas on September 23, 2021," the motion is denied because Lucas served responses. Ballonoff's grievance is, in fact, that the responses were inadequate. Second, to the extent Ballonoff requests an order directing Lucas to file verified responses without objections, the motion is denied. The issue is the timeliness of Lucass response to Ballonoffs discovery demand. "The party making a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling shall serve a copy of the demand on the party to whom it is directed and on all other parties who have appeared in the action." (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.040.) Ballonoff failed to serve the party on whom the demand was directed. (Code Civ. Proc., 2031.040.) The POS, included as Attachment A to the motion, shows Ballonoff served Franchesca Callejo, who is Lucas counsel in his capacity as trustee; and Charles Jonas, attorney for Silverman-Ballonoff. Ballonoff did not serve Vincent Yu, Lucas separate litigation counsel. Had Ballonoff properly served Lucas with the discovery at issue, his responses would have been due on October 29, 2021. To the extent Lucas only became aware of the served discovery sometime in mid-October 2021 and served responses shortly thereafter on November 5, 2021, the Court finds the responses are timely. Ballonoffs objections under sections 2031.220, 2031.230 and 2031.240 are overruled. Ballonoff has not carried her burden of showing violations of the relevant statutes, nor has she demonstrated any prejudice from any claimed violation.
